data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook: Fragile Optimism Stands In Equities; What's Next?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

US stock benchmarks are still attempting to price a cautious but seemingly better narrative around the Middle East conflict into another rebound in today's session.

This continues the theme that had shaken markets in Monday's chaotic but more positive weekly open: the war doesn't seem to be taking a turn for the worse, hinting at what could really be a four to five-week-long conflict.

It would be almost too good to be true to see Donald Trump make good on his words, having announced such a deadline to the ceaseless strikes on Islamic regime targets in Iran.

The current war isn't faring well with the American public, and right ahead of the midterms coming up in November, the president surely doesn't want to aggravate his case.

But during wartime, each headline has to be taken with a pinch of salt, particularly with Iran reportedly rejecting Trump's 15-point plan to add their own demands.

They are particularly keen on conserving their long-range ballistic missile capabilities, but the world knows how dangerous they have been, so expect this to be a zone of contention. We should learn more about these developments by Friday.

Despite all the uncertainty, traders should focus more on what is happening in front of them: the oil stocks' inverse correlation is the only clear market development since the beginning of the month. Tracking the commodity will act as a guide to navigate this clouded environment (while headlines do the exact opposite).

WTI oil had gapped lower in recent sessions and held a lower trajectory since, but has stalled its descent around the $88 level, and this has weighed on stock markets since the mid-session.

In today's action, all stock indexes are moving together with no clear outperformer. See more just below. Let's spot where today's cautiously optimistic price action is heading by looking at today’s intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The rebound hasn't seen any consistent pattern since Monday, except for the previous session's manufacturing outperformance – process industries and pharmaceuticals are the only sectors flashing green in today's session. The rest is broadly chaotic, so for individual stock traders, the best is to look for local ranges to play.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

For the first time since February 26, the Dow Jones is trading above its 4H 50-period moving average, a striking progress, particularly with the consistent downtrend that had developed since.

Combined with a weak but persistent exit from the March bear channel, it seems that the DJIA is only a few positive headlines from a rebound. 48,000 would be a decent target in that event. Above 48,000, expect the rebound to hold towards 50,000.

The only issue is that optimism in such an environment could prove short-lived; hence, with bullish views in the market, make sure that your size is under control to allow for more flexibility in case things turn sour again. Any session close below 46,300 would continue the downtrend.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Resistance 47,000 +/- 100 points (session highs and major resistance)
  • Momentum resistance 47,500 to 47,650
  • Key resistance at 48,000
  • 48,400 to 48,500 mini resistance

Support Levels

  • 4H 50-period MA 46,437
  • March 8 war lows pivot 46,200 to 46,300.
  • January 2025 highs: 45,000 to 45,280 (Monday lows)

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is actually looking more pessimistic, failing to persistently hold above 24,200 with its RSI actually turning bearish. Breaking 24,150 could see a quick test of the 23,800 support. Even in the event of the rebound, it looks like the tech index has less inherent strength and could lag on a rally.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • 24,387 4H 50-period
  • 24,450 to 25,550 range pivot (short-term resistance)
  • Mini intraday resistance 24,750
  • Key resistance 25,000 to 25,200 (range highs – long-term bullish above)

Support Levels

  • February support: 24,150
  • October-November support: 23,800 to 24,000
  • Morning lows: August 2025 next support at 23,580 to 23,700
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 looks right in the middle between bullish and bearish; it remains the index to trade if you prefer less brutal up-and-down swings. As long as the price action remains above 6,580, the outlook isn't bearish, but not so bullish either:

  • Buyers will want a break above 6,650 to relaunch a bullish outlook.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Momentum pivot: 6,640 to 6,650
  • 6,680 to 6,700 mini resistance
  • 6,740 key intraday resistance
  • Pivotal resistance 6,770 to 6,800

Support Levels

  • 6,570 to 6,600 Monday key double bottom support
  • 6,490 to 6,512 October lows
  • 6,442 morning lows
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades, and keep track of WTI prices!

data.title: Janus Henderson Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 2.02% and the Russell Midcap® Value Index returned 1.42%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the real estate and financials sectors contributed to relative performance, while stock selection in the information technology and consumer discretionary sectors detracted.

Outlook

We remain confident in our disciplined value investment approach even as we see potential sources of market volatility.

Investment environment

  • Mid-cap stocks rose on positive earnings news, Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts, continued strength in stocks with exposure to artificial intelligence (AI), and optimism that economic growth may prove stronger than anticipated.
  • Given the protracted government shutdown, economic data was sparse except for company commentary and private investment surveys, which pointed to a slowly improving economic environment.
  • While investors paid more attention to fundamentals relative to earlier in the year, we continued to see pockets of outperformance by lower-quality stocks, as non-earners continued to outperform.

Portfolio review

We were pleased with our outperformance during the quarter, that was driven partly by our position in Freeport-McMoRan. Shares of the copper and gold producer rallied in the quarter as both copper and gold prices soared. The company reached a favorable agreement with the Indonesian government, ceding some control of one of its projects in return for a long-term operating license. We believe the company remains well positioned to benefit from higher copper prices, given continued strong demand and constrained industry supply. However, we trimmed our position on strength.

Kirby Corporation, another relative contributor, transports petroleum and petrochemical products. It also operates an engine overhaul business, which has seen strong growth due to the rapid buildout in U.S. data center capacity. After underperforming in the third quarter owing to weaker petrochemical volumes, the stock moved higher in the fourth quarter, as the company became viewed as an AI beneficiary. We continue to hold a sizeable position in Kirby.

Globus Medical was a relative contributor in the healthcare sector. The medical device-maker delivered strong execution and improved profitability, supported by synergies from recent acquisitions. Robust sales of spinal products resulted in better-than-expected third-quarter earnings growth and an improved outlook.

Relative performance was hindered by our position in Bath & Body Works (BBWI). The specialty retailer reported disappointing revenue growth and reduced guidance, reflecting both cautious spending by its consumers as well as some operational issues that need to be addressed. Despite a very inexpensive valuation, we have significantly reduced our holdings given our concerns over poor execution by company management, and because we believe the next iteration of their turnaround will take considerable time.

Owens Corning was another detractor. We invested in the roofing and building products supplier because of its attractive valuation, which we believed more than priced in the risk of continued weakness in new home starts. However, a less severe storm season also resulted in fewer roof repairs, further dampening the company's revenue growth. Given near-term uncertainty for the business, we exited the position.

NICE Systems was also a detractor. The information technology company provides software-based call center solutions, and it has faced concerns over possible competitive weakness due to accelerating use of AI. The company has tried to address these concerns through an acquisition and its own investments in AI, but the cost of these initiatives led to reduced margin guidance. We sold the position as we saw a less favorable balance of risk and potential reward.

The portfolio ended the quarter overweight in the materials, financials, healthcare, and industrials sectors. It was underweight in the consumer staples, real estate, information technology, energy, utilities, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors.

Manager outlook

We remain cautiously optimistic about the market in 2026. The economy continues to grow at a steady pace, and we may see modest improvement in cyclical sectors such as transportation, industrials, and materials. While weaker employment growth and above-average inflation have

pressured consumer spending by lower-income households, this slowdown has been offset by resilient spending from higher-income households benefiting from equity price appreciation. We also see potential for additional Fed rate cuts and fiscal stimulus that could provide a tailwind for economic growth.

At the same time, many companies remain cautious in their hiring and capital spending plans. Valuations in the equity market do not appear cheap by historical standards, but we have continued to find areas of opportunity, including healthcare. Recently, the market has experienced increased volatility around the AI trade: on some days, excitement around AI drives market returns, while on other days investor concerns lead to a recalibration. We would expect this dynamic to continue as we head into 2026. As always, we remain on the lookout for opportunities to use volatility to our advantage as we seek out high-quality opportunities yet to be recognized by other investors. We remain focused on investing in well-managed, profitable companies with low debt levels that we believe are undervalued by the market. In our view, such companies can outperform regardless of the economic backdrop.

We thank you for your continued co-investment in the Janus Henderson Mid Cap Value Fund.

data.title: Vertical Aerospace: Liquidity Crisis Deepens As Transition Delay Triggers Dilution Spiral
data.description:
Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) has lost 47% since my strong sell rating in July 2025. The stock is now down 84.8% since I initiated with a sell rating. As I discuss in this report, Vertical Aerospace is not necessarily a company that does not have a promising product, but its liquidity runway generally has been too short for a comfortable investment in a higher-risk space. It simply means that additional delays in certification timelines may cause significant volatility and erode shareholder value and that is exactly what happened with Vertical Aerospace.

Vertical Aerospace Transition Milestone Slips

For eVTOL companies, the key element to success is not providing fancy marketing material but to realistically assess the certification timelines. Not doing so may result in the company not raising sufficient capital or not raising capital timely. Vertical Aerospace is aiming for a certification of its eVTOL by 2028 and cash flow break-even by 2030. In September 2025, the company highlighted that it expects to have a positive free cash flow by 2030. That is a >$100 million shift from an earlier break-even expectation in 2030. So, that is a positive development that can be seen as a positive for shareholders as it could potentially reduce some of the requirements on capital raises.

Vertical Aerospace stock tumbled following its Q4 earnings release. In my view there was nothing major that would justify a drop. The company did highlight that adverse weather conditions did impact the test campaign, which is perfectly normal and they also said during the Q4 2025 earnings call that the transition from vertical lift/hover to wing-born flight is taking longer than expected:

So the focus right now from the entire organization is to complete that transition successfully and as quickly as possible from where we stand today. And the reality is it's taken us longer than we anticipated. On our last earnings call, I specifically said it's weeks, not months. Well, actually, as I was thinking about it this morning, it's months, not weeks. But we are very, very close at this juncture.

I believe that this was the main reason for the stock price to decline. Basically, what we heard during the call was that management revised the prior statement of transition flight being weeks away rather than months to months away rather than weeks. I do not believe that they mean months from now, but months from the prior call.

Transition flight is a major milestone to attract capital and strategic investments. So, if that milestone shifts to the right the strategic investments also slide to the right. Some investors have hammered the partnership with Honeywell as a big item that would invalidate my prior short thesis. Investors are not wrong to think that Honeywell could make a substantial investment. However, what we see now is that as timelines shifts, so do the discussions on strategic investments which often still mean that shares will be issued.

While I am not ignoring the strategic investment that Honeywell could commit to, I am wary of chaining too many events together to sculpt a bull thesis as it also means that if the links of the chain of events breaks somewhere there is substantial downside and that is essentially what we have seen happening now. It is one of the major risks for eVTOL manufacturers and their investors. This is emerging technology that is being turned into a commercial product and that takes time, effort and money with occasional setbacks. If you paint too rosy of a picture with a near-perfect path, as a company you may not raise enough cash timely and as an investor you risk buying shares too early. In the same way, we saw people pointing at backlogs while it should be known that backlogs at this stage do not mean much. In many cases the backlog are letter of intent and not firm purchase orders and even if they are pre-delivery payments will only start flowing in when we have a line of sight on deliveries which we currently have not. So, definitely in the cash intensive test and certification phase there is no meaningful expectations of pre-delivery payments and we also note that the earliest deliveries will be loss making as is typical for aerospace products and ramping the production is going to first of all reduce the production costs on future produces units and also drive some pre-delivery payments. In other words, the faster the ramp up the more cash a company can start receiving which could at least partially offset cash burns on earlier productions.

The share price decline in my view is not so much driven by Vertical Aerospace having a major slip, but shareholders simply did not properly weigh the risks.

A Look At Vertical Aerospace’s Liquidity Runway

Vertical Aerospace ended the year with $92.6 million in cash and around $40 million in receivables with no payables outstanding. So, that is a pro-forma cash pile of roughly $130 million excluding $1.6 million in restricted cash. During the year, the company burned through nearly $111.2 million of cash while raising $186.8 million. So, the entire cash burn and current cash position was raised through issuance of shares and warrant exercises. The company expects to burn around $190-$200 million in cash over the next 12 months measures from the 31st of December 2025. Linearized, that means that the company is funded for around 8 months ahead which is not an extremely comfortable position. Typically, I believe that a company should be funded more than 12 months ahead and 18-24 months being a comfortable spot. That is not the case for Vertical Aerospace and that may also induce higher volatility when milestones are slipping. Vertical Aerospace missed the opportunity to enter the year with stronger funding coverage.

This year and over the next two years, the cash burn is expected to increase as the company goes through the process of flight testing, building a conforming prototype, certification and early production ramp up. So, the free cash flow burn is expected to increase with the burn peaking in 2028 and the capital raise peaking in 2027 as I believe the company should not only raise more to cover its in-year free cash flow burn but also increase the liquidity runway towards 12 months. That indicates that around $1.1 billion will still be raised from shareholders and possibly $1 billion if we assume that the company can also comfortably execute production increases with a $200 million cash balance.

At a current price of $2.65 per share, it would mean that another 411 million shares or so need to be issued against a share count of roughly 90 million at the end of 2025. At a current price of $2.65, the stock is trading substantially below the strike prices of $6 for the Tranche A warrants and $7.50 for the Tranche B warrants (Page 85, Section Outstanding Warrants). So, warrant exercises are unlikely.

In my view, Vertical Aerospace has made things extremely difficult for itself by having an extremely short liquidity runway and combined with delays on the transition milestone that has left the company in a position where share prices are low and capital needs to fund the accelerated cash burn and increased forward visibility have increased. The company essentially didn’t fund itself timely. It exited 2025 with 7-8 months visibility, which is a 4-5 months shortfall. If they more or less double the raise this year, they can get to 10 months and 12 months in the year after, but share prices have already fallen. So, they are now in a position where raising cash is going to hurt shareholders a lot.

Currently, the company is trading at roughly 2x cash per share and against that multiple, we would see fundamental support for the share prices declined from $2.70 to $1.10 by 2029. The company exited the year trading at almost 4x cash per share. In that scenario the valuation by 2029 would be $4.50. The share price has now fallen 50% year-to-date and that does not create a setting in which we can say that the share price decline exceeded the downside thereby creating an attractive opportunity. The reason is that to raise the same amount of cash, the company now needs to issue more shares.

So, Vertical Aerospace shareholders are facing higher dilution risks as the stock price has dropped at a time the company was already behind on building its liquidity runway creating a dilution spiral for the company. What the company has to hope for is that investor sentiment improves and eVTOL peers have some sort of good news that lifts the entire peer group allowing Vertical Aerospace to raise capital at more favorable dilution for investors. Vertical Aerospace could partially provide that bullish milestone itself by achieving transition flight, but the question remains how much the stock can rebound. If the stock would double on the news of transition flight being achieved, there would be dilution but at more favorable terms. So, there is somewhat of a buy angle for investors with an extremely high risk tolerance. If you would buy at these levels and the stock price increases again to roughly the 4x cash per share, we would end up with $5.20 marking a 100% gain and from there, further dilution would drive the price down to $3.29 in the years ahead. So, from $2.65 now to $3.29 in the years ahead, we would be looking at a 24% gain. However, at current prices, the support level would be at $2.55 indicating another 4% downside for this year and 28% downside with capital raises needed next year.

So, the current stock price of $2.65 is not a comfortable one. If we look at when the company would have 12 months of cash on the balance sheet, we would be looking at 2027 at which point the supported price target is $1.83 which is also the point at which the strong sell rating would likely disappear.

Conclusion: EVTL Stock Remains A Strong Sell

There definitely is an angle for investors with a high-risk tolerance. However, that would more be an angle of betting on a strong rebound in the stock price when transition flight is achieved and that would be highly opportunistic. Whether the stock price strongly recovers or not, as long as the company does not have 12 months of cash on the balance sheet it remains too high risk for serious investors with a willingness to engage in speculative opportunities. Whether it is at $5 per share or $2.65 per share, the company has to raise another $1 billion before it gets to the break-even point on free cash flow.

I believe that Vertical Aerospace has missed the opportunity to fund itself for the next 12 months before its capital needs start to accelerate. In other words, the company has pushed expansion of the liquidity runway too far ahead in time which either is a genius call if the development is going smooth and ahead of schedule, but it becomes a major risk when there are delays, even when those delays are extremely small. Vertical Aerospace has found that out the hard way in a market setting that is not favouring higher risk names.

data.title: Fidelity Select Consumer Staples Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The consumer staples sector returned -0.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 Index, underperforming the 2.66% gain of the broad-based S&P 500® index.

In Q4, stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large-caps had only a slight advantage over small-caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

Within this environment, the underperformance of the consumer staples sector was largely due to a modest rotation away from defensive sectors toward cyclical and higher-growth stocks. The MSCI consumer staples index returned -2.64% in October, whereas the S&P 500® notched a 2.34% gain. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting. Staples stocks then gained in November (+3.77%) before losing steam in December (-1.68%), when the focus returned to a U.S. economy largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech companies. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10.

Among the biggest segments in the sector, consumer staples merchandise retail (+3%), making up 31% of the index, gained ground, aided by some of its largest components, including Walmart (WMT) (+8%) and Target (TGT) (+10%). Soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages (+7%), making up about 18% of the index, also helped the sector's result, with Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) (+10%) and Coca Cola (+6%) leading the way. Conversely, household products, constituting roughly 16% of the benchmark, returned -7%, hindered by Kimberly Clark (KMB) (-18%) and Energizer (ENR) Holdings (-19%). Packaged foods & meats (-6%) also lagged, with underperformers including Mondelez International (MDLZ) (-13%) and JM Smucker (SJM) (-9%). ■

* 1 basis point = 0.01%.

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 1.96%, outpacing the -0.66% result of the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 Index.

Both industry positioning and security selection contributed to the fund's performance versus the sector index for the quarter, especially picks within personal care products and positioning in soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages.

From the latter group, an outsized stake in Keurig Dr Pepper (+10%) was the biggest individual relative contributor, as the stock rebalanced the past three months following a late-August plunge after the firm's announcement of a deal to buy JDE Peet's for nearly $18 billion, a move intended to bolster its coffee business. Though we viewed the deal as strategically questionable, we remained bullish on Keurig Dr Pepper. The company was our fourth-largest holding, at approximately 8% of total fund assets, and the top overweight position at year-end, though we modestly reduced the holding in Q4.

From the tobacco group, avoiding tobacco product producer and sizable benchmark component Altria (-11%) helped relative performance. The stock fell after weaker-than-expected Q3 sales reported in late October. We have avoided Altria, preferring other tobacco firms, specifically British American Tobacco (+10%), a non-index holding that contributed for the quarter.

Conversely, positioning in consumer staples merchandise retail detracted most, followed by picks in household products. A timely overweight stake in battery manufacturer Energizer (-19%) was the biggest individual detractor. The company's shares fell after the firm reported weak guidance for quarterly adjusted earnings per share and gross margin. When the stock was down earlier in the year, we built a large stake in Energizer because we viewed it as a potential value play for the fund, as it was quite cheap despite steady financial performance. We still hold this view, and it was the fund's No. 9 position as of year-end. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As the new year begins, we believe the sector could benefit from an improved investment environment, driven by lower interest rates and moderating inflation. A key development will be the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, a massive tax and spending package that passed in July. Many key provisions will take effect in 2026, and we expect them to deliver a significant income boost to middle-income consumers and potentially translate into stronger discretionary spending.

In addition, several sector-specific challenges appear to be stabilizing. For example, we believe the alcohol-consumption reset among certain segments of the population has largely played out, reducing the likelihood of further downside in sector industries related to alcohol. Similarly, the headwind for certain food and beverage segments, due to the rapid adoption of GLP-1 drugs, is subsiding. In our view, these factors suggest a more balanced operating backdrop for consumer staples companies in 2026, with fewer disruptive dynamics and improved consumer spending.

Valuations for stocks in the sector remain compelling, based on our analysis, especially relative to segments that are heavily exposed to AI. While the sector's performance in 2026 will hinge to some extent on broader market dynamics, we believe the risk-reward picture within staples appears attractive heading into the new year. Overall, we see a more constructive backdrop for consumer staples stocks, with support from fiscal stimulus, easing headwinds and compelling valuations. With the sector offering rich opportunities for stock selection, we are focused on undervalued names and turnaround stories, especially those with stabilizing business fundamentals and high potential for mean reversion.

Regardless of the market backdrop, we expect the fund's positioning and our core investment strategy to remain unchanged. We will continue to focus on distinguishing between relative "winners" and "losers" within each industry. This stock selection process is based on building robust financial and operating models that we believe give us an edge in forecasting earnings revisions for companies. We supplement this fundamental work with industry-level analysis to get ahead of broader trends that could influence the sector.

In managing the fund, we also plan to focus on core fundamentals and traditional industry drivers, with a preference for companies operating in a favorable market structure and maintaining a strong underlying growth profile. We also continue to be optimistic about stocks that we consider undervalued because investors have overreacted to company-specific challenges. For example, Mondelez International was pressured by the rising cost of cocoa in 2025, but we foresee the potential for profit-margin recovery now that costs for the multinational snack maker have eased.

Other value-oriented stocks we like include Kenvue, maker of Tylenol, which has been hurt by recent product safety claims. The stock is extremely cheap, based on our estimate, and with Kimberly-Clark recently announcing plans to acquire the company, we see a clear path to improved performance in 2026. We increased the positions in Mondelez and Kenvue in Q4, and both are among the portfolio's top-10 holdings at year-end.

Meanwhile, we maintained the fund's large overweight positions in soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages and distillers & vintners. We believe the beverages segment, particularly non-alcoholic beverages, is the best market structure in consumer staples, based on its high barriers to entry, relatively tepid competitive pressure and fragmented customer base. ■

Investment Approach

• Fidelity® Select Consumer Staples Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.

• Our core philosophy is that stock prices follow earnings and returns over the long term. We look for durable franchises capable of compounding value and delivering above-average earnings growth over time.

• Investment opportunities arise when there is a differentiated view with regard to the long-term earnings power of a company. We focus on stocks where the market underestimates the magnitude or duration of growth and the valuation is attractive.

• Fundamental research is key to our approach, as we build the portfolio stock by stock and take a multiyear investment perspective to get a differentiated view on long-term performance drivers. We also look to capitalize on short-term market opportunities, with the goal of optimizing risk-adjusted returns for shareholders.

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Gas Prices Up More Than $1 In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Gas prices have climbed for the tenth consecutive week, marking the longest streak of increases since early 2022 and pushing prices to their highest point since August of that year. As of March 23rd, weekly prices jumped 24 cents for regular and 25 cents for premium. Throughout the month of March, regular gas has surged by a total of $1.02, while premium is up $1.03.

Currently, the national average for regular gasoline stands at $3.96 per gallon, with premium averaging $4.94. These prices represent a significant jump since the start of the year, with regular gas up 41% and premium up 31%. Compared to one year ago, regular gas is 27% more expensive (up from $3.12), while premium has seen a 22.5% increase (up from $4.03).

Gas Prices by State

According to GasBuddy, regional price disparities have widened significantly, leaving a $2.58 gap between the nation’s highest and lowest averages. California currently holds the highest average for regular gas at $5.81, while Oklahoma offers the cheapest at $3.23. While every state in the nation is now averaging over $3.00 per gallon, a clear geographic divide has emerged.

The West Coast remains the most expensive region, with California, Washington, and Hawaii all surpassing the $5.00 mark. Additionally, there are currently nine states across the West and Northeast that fall into the $4.00 to $5.00 range. Conversely, the lowest prices continue to be found across the South and Central Plains, where state averages remain well below the national average.

Behind the Price: Components and Inflation

To understand these surges, it is helpful to look at the "price at the pump" as a sum of its parts. The retail price of gas is made up of four main components: 1) cost of crude oil, 2) refining costs and profits 3) distribution and marketing costs and profits, and 4) taxes.

The volatility in these components, particularly crude oil, has been clearly reflected in recent years in both the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE). In the following chart, we take a look at gasoline prices adjusted for inflation to show the "real" cost to consumers.

For an easier way to visualize these effects, this next chart overlays the nominal price with the inflation-adjusted "real" price for both regular and premium gasoline.

Crude Oil Market

This next chart shows the West Texas light crude oil price over the past 8 months. As of March 23rd, WTIC end-of-day spot price for crude oil closed at $88.87, a 5.0% decrease from the previous week and the first decline since mid-February.

The final chart provides a monthly overlay of West Texas light crude, Brent crude, and unleaded gasoline end-of-day spot prices, showing the tight correlation between global benchmarks and domestic retail costs.

ETFs associated with gasoline, WTIC crude oil, and Brent crude oil include United States Gasoline Fund LP (UGA), United States Oil Fund LP (USO), United States Brent Oil Fund LP (BNO), and Invesco DB Oil Fund (DBO).

data.title: 3 Winners From Micron's Huge Capex Guide
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last week was always going to be a big week for Nvidia (NVDA) and its ecosystem with the GTC26 event, but also for Micron (MU), which is one of the essential memory suppliers for Nvidia’s chips.

That’s because Micron would also be reporting its Q2 FY26 quarter a couple of days after Nvidia’s GTC event.

Micron’s shares should have catapulted higher on blowout numbers in its Q2 ER, but markets clearly used those earnings results to create winners, and Micron’s capex was the trigger for markets.

Markets believe some WFE (wafer equipment) manufacturers/vendors will become big beneficiaries of Micron’s surging capex over the next year as shown in the chart below.

Exhibit A: Quarterly revenue growth rates for Micron since 1998.

I believe markets are right, and I explain below why Lam Research (NASDAQ:LRCX), Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT), and KLA Corp. (NASDAQ:KLAC) will benefit from Micron’s rising capex as well as elevated capex plans from Micron’s direct peers.

Primer On Micron’s Capex Plans Vs. The Memory Industry

Markets have clearly demonstrated their disapproval for Micron’s forward-looking capex forecasts, as seen in Exhibit A above.

A few days ago, I published this post explaining key details about Micron’s capex components. In my strong view, understanding this nuance about the memory maker’s capex is essential for investors when it comes to investing in WFE vendors.

As I noted in that post, there are 3 components to the capex budgets of most memory makers like Micron—WFE equipment, construction, and test/assembly. Micron’s management usually guides investors on its capex plans in dollar terms and the areas /components that management intends to spend on.

Markets are very appreciative of management when the focus on Micron’s capex is/will be directly towards WFE to support the production of its advanced memory products/components, especially for HBM and next-gen DRAM nodes. Spending on EUV (extreme ultraviolet) lithography is one example of WFE capex, which goes directly into developing more advanced technology manufacturing process nodes like the 1-γ node for DRAM and the G9 node for 3D NAND.

However, on the Q2 call, Micron’s management guided up FY26 capex to $25B, up from prior estimates of $18B, and simultaneously noted that this 39% sequential increase in the FY26 capex outlook would be “driven by cleanroom facility-related CapEx,” like the Tonglou, Taiwan location. This would be “followed by a construction spend increase in our U.S. fab projects" in FY26.

That’s not all. Management also revealed construction capex would see a ~$10B increase in FY27:

We project our fiscal 2027 CapEx to step up meaningfully to support HBM- and DRAM-related investments. We expect construction-related CapEx to increase by over $10 billion year-over-year in fiscal 2027 as we build out our global manufacturing sites to address long-term demand opportunities. In addition, we expect higher equipment spend year-over-year in fiscal 2027.

This clearly demarcates some key beneficiaries from not just Micron’s elevated focus on construction capex but also from rising construction-focused capex from Micron’s peers like Samsung and SK Hynix.

Here are my winners from the elevated construction-focused capex being seen among the three largest memory makers in the world, as shown below.

1. Lam Research: Strong Support For Cleanrooms

Such is the strength in the WFE space that Lam Research’s management believes the entire WFE industry’s TAM can grow 22% in CY26 to $135B. These growth estimates are still likely conservative since they see a “shortage of available clean room space.”

Typically, Lam Research is known to provide products and solutions that help chip foundries like Micron during initial chip fabrication stages of deposition and etching. Lam Research is most known for its Systems division, which houses its deposition and etching solutions.

But during the stages of setting up clean rooms, like I talked about in the previous section, a large volume of resources are allocated towards constructing chip fabrication shells. Lam Research engages chip foundries early on during the construction stages in areas like facility planning, WFE transport, and advanced WFE installation. And Lam Research engages customers like Micron and peers via its Customer Support business group, which brought in $1.68B in the December quarter. This business group began accelerating sequentially, growing 13.5% in December, versus the sluggish growth through Q2 and Q3 quarters.

This all-round growing strength in Lam Research’s business made management up their outlook at a recent conference this month, where they said end demand “is stronger than” the prior $135B forecast, underlining a prior outlook that H2 CY26 will be strong for the company as Lam Research benefits from elevated construction capex.

2. Applied Materials: AGS Growth Vector

I have written about Applied Materials before and switched from being bullish to neutral. But that was 15 months ago, so a refresh to Applied Materials is needed.

Just like Lam Research, Applied Materials expects a strong pickup in growth in H2 this year, and a shortage of enough cleanrooms will be “a key factor pacing the rate of [WFE] investment,” according to management.

Applied Materials has two key revenue segments, Semiconductor Systems and AGS (Applied Global Services), that each account for ~47-48% of the overall business. Via AGS, Applied Materials engages with customers by providing on-field site engineers to help design, plan, and support WFE setup and installation. Customers also use Applied Materials' AIx tool for virtual fab mapping and create interactive digital twins of the new foundry layout, allowing customers like Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix to simulate WFE placement, maximize clean room space, and remove potential bottlenecks before WFEs and machines are physically installed.

3. KLA Corp: Semi Process Control

Unlike Lam Research and Applied Materials, KLA Corp focuses on providing semiconductor process control and process-enabling solutions. Process control solutions like its inspection, quality control, and chip yield management solutions account for 90% or more of its revenues.

As new cleanroom facilities are being set up or existing facilities are being extended for more capacity, KLA’s tools are typically deployed to monitor the cleanliness of the new fabrication environment, ensuring that air quality and foundry environmental conditions meet standards set by customers like Micron before the WFE is installed and during the operation of WFE. KLA also deploys their onsite engineers routinely on behalf of the customer at the customer fab site to support the installation of advanced process control as expensive WFE from companies like ASML get installed in these cleanrooms.

The big difference between KLA and the other two peers that I mentioned above is that KLA operates at strong gross margins that stand apart from Applied Materials and Lam Research, which makes KLA Corp. a lot more interesting in this era of elevated construction capex seen among broader foundry companies and memory companies.

Recently, KLA Corp extended their current share buyback program from the $3.9B that still exists by an incremental $7B. The total value of their buyback program represents ~5% of KLA’s market cap.

Valuations May Be Stretched But Don’t Factor Coming Upside

All three WFE companies follow Micron’s FY system that ends in August. That means all three

The shares of all three capex beneficiaries I noted above have already run up in anticipation of the coming capex boom from their target customers. This can be seen in elevated forward revenue multiples below.

Lam Research is the only company that analysts believe can score 21-23% growth in FY26 and FY27. Compared to forward growth, Lam Research trades at 13x FY26 revenues and 11x FY27 revenues.

In contrast to Lam Research, its other large peer, Applied Materials is expected to grow 11% in FY26 and 18% in FY27 but trades at 9.4x FY26 and just 8x FY27 revenues.

Finally, KLA, the smallest of the three, in terms of market cap, trades at 15x FY26 revenues and 12.7x FY27 revenues for revenue growth projections of 10% this FY that more than doubles to 21.7% in FY27.

Personally, I believe all three companies are still undervalued because there is still strong scope for revenue estimates to keep increasing. For example, FY27 revenue growth estimates for all three companies were hiked by 10-15% in just the last 3 months as per SA data. So there is definitely more upside in the shares of KLA, Lam Research, and Applied Materials.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

Two main risks when investing in the WFE companies I noted in this post.

First, all companies have some amount of revenue exposure to China, and that introduces the risk of possible regulatory oversight and the added vulnerability of tariffs/export restrictions that could return.

Two, Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA are highly sensitive to the capex budgets of their target customers, which introduces a different kind of business cyclicality and capex volatility. So if their customers suddenly see any weakness in their own businesses, they may scale back capex, which may hurt all three beneficiaries.

Takeaway

Micron’s ballooning capex is a direct reaction function to the unprecedented demand environment seen in the memory market.

While that benefits Micron long-term, the new capacity that Micron and its peers are rushing to create will make strong beneficiaries out of WFE vendors such as Applied Materials, KLA Corp., and Lam Research that are best positioned to assist each foundry in the global race to construct more clean rooms.

I am bullish on the shares of Applied Materials, KLA Corp and Lam Research.

data.title: No Need To Rush Into Arrow Financial
data.description:
Just a couple of days ago, I finished an analysis of Greene County Bancorp (GCBC). I rated the stock as a Buy based on its strong earnings growth, credit quality, and operational metrics. My research piqued my curiosity about other small-cap banks in the area and led me to learn more about Arrow Financial Corporation (AROW). With a gain of over 22% in the last 12 months, AROW has certainly made some meaningful improvements to its bottom line in the last year that have caught the market’s attention. Its Q4 2025 results included a record net income of $14 million, or $0.85 per share.

A deeper dive into the company’s operations, however, paints a noticeable contrast to many of the positives that I saw in GCBC. Several of Arrow's key barometers for performance fall short of the usual standards I want to see in a small-cap bank before deeming it a Buy. The company also recently made an offer to acquire privately held Adirondack Bancorp in a deal that is valued at about $89 million. Reading through the terms of the deal and the assets that AROW will acquire did not spark any new enthusiasm on my end. I consider Arrow Financial to be a Hold for the reasons discussed below.

Company Overview

With a geographic footprint of 38 branches in Northeast New York, Arrow Financial is the holding company for Arrow Bank. Formerly operating under the names Glens Falls National Bank and Trust Company and Saratoga National Bank and Trust Company, Arrow is a full-service commercial bank that also offers wealth management and insurance services to its communities. In 2024, it was named to Piper Sandler’s Small-Cap All-Stars, a list of high-quality banks that included Greene County Bancorp in 2025.

AROW has a current market cap of $550.2 million. According to its 2025 10-K filing, the company controlled $4.45 billion in assets at the end of last year, including a loan portfolio worth $3.45 billion. The bank’s total gross loans only grew by 1.7% in FY 2025.

Further detail regarding the company’s loan mix can be seen below. Unlike many other small regional banks that are heavily exposed to commercial real estate, Arrow’s largest loan allocation belongs to home mortgages, which make up 40% of the total portfolio. Consumer loans are the next largest segment at approximately 31%. These loans are primarily auto loans that were originated through dealerships in New York and Vermont. I am generally less enthusiastic about investing in companies with high exposure to auto loan debt, as delinquency rates are often about twice what they are for home mortgages. New York State has an auto loan delinquency rate of 4.8%, which places it in the middle of the pack compared to other states in the US.

Total deposits held by the bank were valued at $3.94 billion at the end of last year. This was a 2.9% increase from the end of 2024. Non-interest-bearing deposits comprised $722 million, or 18.3%, of that most recent total. An additional $850 million was held in interest-bearing checking accounts that paid less than 1% in interest in Q4. The bank’s overall cost of deposits is relatively in line with the national average for a bank of its size.

Arrow Financial currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share. The company has recorded eight straight years of dividend growth. AROW is on its way to nine years of higher payouts after increasing its distribution in Q1 2026 by 3.4%. The stock has a forward yield of 3.6%.

Analysis Of Arrow Financial

As mentioned in my introduction, Q4 2025 was a record quarter for net income for Arrow Financial, and that strong bottom-line performance helped the company achieve earnings per share of $2.65 for FY 2025. Net income of $44 million for the full year was 48.1% better than the $29.7 million earned in FY 2024. The biggest driver in this improvement was an increase in net interest income of $21.4 million over the last 12 months. Loan growth and higher rates charged on those loans helped increase interest income while the bank was paying lower interest on deposits as savings rates declined.

Even as these trends were certainly all positive for the bank, AROW is still below average in its net interest margin. The bank’s NIM of 3.24% is impacted by the higher rates paid on municipal deposits. As shown in the chart below that details all of the funding sources for Arrow Bank, $787 million, or 19.8%, of the bank’s deposits are from municipal depositors.

Like its net interest margin, other performance metrics for the bank are just below or right around average when compared to similar-sized banks. AROW’s return on assets in Q4 2025 was 1.24%, a significant improvement from Q4 2024, when the number stood at just 0.41%. During that final quarter of the previous year, the company booked a number of non-recurring charges, including a $3 million loss on the sale of investment securities. Arrow Financial’s efficiency ratio was 59.0% at the end of Q4 2025, 42 basis points higher than its peer mean. Again, this number has been showing improvement, which would lead me to be more optimistic about the year ahead.

One statistic that has not been improving for the bank is its share of delinquent loans. As the graph below shows, the ratio of delinquencies to total loans has been increasing each quarter since Q1 2025. It ended the year at 0.84%, its highest level in four years. Net charge-offs at the end of last year were 0.19%, which is 20 basis points better than average but 10 basis points higher than at the end of 2024. Non-performing loans stood at just 0.24% at the end of the year, and the bank’s allowance of credit losses coverage seemed more than able to manage any problems in the short term.

Overall, my assessment of Arrow Financial as a stand-alone entity is that the company is showing good momentum in some areas but still has room for improvement. Its stock trades at a forward P/E of 9.1, which seems reasonable for its operations and recent performance. The company's tangible book value per share of $24.71 was a 10.3% improvement from the end of 2024. Its stock currently trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.35. Without the addition of its soon-to-be-acquired assets, I put Arrow Financial in the "good, but not great" category for small-cap regional banks.

Analysis Of Adirondack Bancorp Acquisition

Adirondack Bancorp is the holding company for Adirondack Bank, which operates 19 branch locations and one additional loan production office in Upstate and Central New York. The company reported just $942 million in assets at the end of 2025 after crossing over the $1 billion threshold at the end of Q3 2025. A pro forma look at what the combination of Adirondack and Arrow could look like, both from a geographical and financial standpoint, is given below.

On the surface, the geographical additions from the acquisition may be more valuable than the actual assets. Micron Technology recently started the construction of a chip-making plant just north of Syracuse that could add as many as 9000 jobs in the region. Last April, yogurt producer Chobani broke ground on a $1.2 billion plant in Rome, NY, that could create an additional 1000 jobs. News items like these give hope for economic expansion in the region that could benefit the newly acquired branches from Adirondack Bank.

From the standpoint of its current operations, Adirondack looks somewhat less appealing. The bank’s return on assets and return on equity ratios, which were at 0.52% and 6.97%, respectively, at the end of Q4 2025, are well below the average for a small community bank. Its most recently reported net interest margin was two basis points below the national average of 3.96%. Adirondack also has a Tier 1 leverage ratio of 8.54%, which is below the 9% standard that I would prefer in a bank of this size.

The pro forma loan and deposit data in the graphic below show the impact of the deal on Arrow Financial. Its yield on loans, which was below average, will increase slightly from 5.47% to 5.50%. Arrow’s high concentration in residential mortgages will grow even more as 47.8% of Adirondack’s loan mix was concentrated in this area. Its cost of deposits will decline 21 basis points as Adirondack has a remarkably low percentage in this area due in large part to 36.5% of its deposits being held in non-interest-bearing accounts.

The deal is being done at a price-to-tangible book value multiple of 1.38, only slightly below the average valuation of all banks in the US but just above the valuation for AROW. Once completed, Arrow Financial is expected to see its own TBV drop by 9.1%, with the earn-back requiring almost three years of time. Arrow estimates that the benefits from the combination of operations will result in an ROA of 1.4% by FY 2027 and a drop in its efficiency rate to 55.1%. The transaction is expected to close in late Q2 2026 or early Q3.

Risks To Consider

Even though the deal for Adirondack Bancorp is relatively small, there are still risks associated with the transaction. The odds of the deal not going through are minimal given the lenient regulatory environment for banks in the US, but that chance does still exist. If the deal is finalized as scheduled, there is the chance that the acquisition will not be as beneficial to Arrow Financial as expected. Growth for the current Adirondack branches may suffer as customers take the merger as an opportunity to shop their accounts with competing banks. The deal will also add about $40 million of goodwill to AROW’s balance sheet.

The second risk to consider before investing in Arrow Financial is its limited geographic diversity within the state of New York. Even after folding in the branches from Adirondack Bancorp, the company will be heavily dependent on the state of New York—its job growth, regulatory environment, and tax policy. New York, like several other high-tax states, is grappling with issues of resident migration to other lower-tax states. Even if New York can keep pace with competing states, the entire financial sector could be in for an extended pullback due to the impact of higher oil prices and higher-than-expected overall inflation in the first half of this year. Just last week, Moody’s top economist stated that the chances of a recession in the US in the next 12 months now stand at 49%.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating currently has a Sell rating on AROW. The stock does receive a “B” in growth, based largely on the earnings improvements that have taken place over the last year. However, it has a “C” in valuation and a failing grade in revisions after the one analyst that covers the company decreased its earnings estimate for FY 2026. I am not one to place too much stock in estimates that are only connected to one single analyst, but this lower estimate was released in February, which could link its cause to the announcement of the Adirondack deal.

The Bottom Line

It is quite possible that the addition of Adirondack to Arrow's operations will be beneficial in the long term, but I do not think it provides justification for a Buy recommendation at this time. My analysis of Arrow by itself is that is it a decent bank, but not a standout. In short, I see no impending catalyst that will cause AROW to perform better than the average small-cap regional bank.

The added stress in the markets that could come from higher inflation, a slowing job market, and the declining odds of a Fed rate cut in the near term could make it more challenging for any regional bank to show meaningful gains in the first half of this year. The 3.6% dividend yield may give some reward to investors who want to hold and wait through the turbulence. However, in my opinion, it is not enough to recommend a buy at this time.

data.title: American Tower: Tenant Default Creates Dip-Buying Opportunity - Diversified 5G/AI Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered American Tower Corporation (AMT) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, thanks to the excellent margin of safety arising from the steep correction/discounted valuations, the reasonable technical indicators, and the robust dual pronged return prospects across capital appreciation/dividend incomes.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the AMT stock here, thanks to the mixed optics from the tenant default/underwhelming FY2026 guidance already triggering the stock price selloff and the consequently cheap valuations/richer dividend yields.

My optimism is further aided by the REIT's diversified growth/monetization tailwinds across the legacy telecom and data center opportunities, during the multi-year 5G/6G/AI-related boom.

AMT's Tenant Headwinds Discussed

Since my last Buy rating, AMT has missed the great market rotation in December 2025, with the telecom/data center REIT seemingly left in the dust, similar to another pure-play telecom REIT, Crown Castle Inc. (CCI).

Part of AMT's headwinds may be attributed to the prior default of DISH Wireless, a tenant that comprises 2% of the REIT's consolidated property revenue and 4% of the UCAN property revenues in FY2025, with it directly contributing to the REIT's impacted FY2026 guidance as the management "continue to pursue legal action to recover the value of its remaining lease obligations."

For reference, AMT has offered an underwhelming FY2026 guidance at:

  • the consolidated organic tenant billings growth at approximately +2% YoY,
  • the adj EBITDA growth at approximately -0.1% YoY,
  • the AFFO per share growth at approximately +1% YoY,

against the FY2025 outperformance at +5.1% YoY, +4.7% YoY, and +8% YoY, respectively.

When compared to AMT's historical top/bottom-line (AFFO per share) growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +5.8%/+5.9% and 10Y CAGR of +8.4%/+7.8%, respectively, I can understand why the market has opted to de-risk first as the stock lost -7.1% of its value post FQ4'25 earnings call.

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that the selloff has been unwarranted, since on an adj basis (after discounting DISH's default), the REIT already expects to deliver a robust adj FY2026 performance at +5% YoY, +5% YoY, and +5% YoY, respectively, with it apparent that the default merely presents a temporary noise in their performance metric.

AMT's Robust 5G/Data Center Monetization Prospects Discussed

1. Telecom/5G/6G Prospects

This is especially since AMT's ongoing capex investment at 2,000 new tower sites is expected to be accretive to its top-line performance by +1% in overall organic tenant billings growth in FY2026.

Given that the global mobile data traffic is expected to grow from 196.7 exabytes per month in 2025 to 483.2 exabytes per month in 2031, expanding at an accelerated CAGR of +16.6%, I am the opinion that its prospects as a leading telecom REIT player is likely to be extremely robust indeed, given the required "doubling in wireless network capacity between now and 2030."

Part of the demand tailwinds are attributed to the increased use of AI at the edge (smart devices) along with the upcoming 5G capacity investments in the EU and emerging international countries - all of which are expected to drive higher mobile data consumption moving forward.

This is on top of the next decade's 6G opportunities, as analysts posited 2028 as "the year of significant advancements, revolutionizing connectivity with unprecedented bandwidth, lightning-fast speed, and ultra-low latency," with it lending credibility to the management's guidance of higher tower activities and organic growth opportunities over the next few years.

2. Data Center Prospects

At the same time, readers cannot forget AMT's double-digit growth prospects from the data center segment, with the robust demand for interconnection-rich colocation deployments already driving the revenue growth by +19% YoY in FQ4'25 (page 31) and the notably richer operating profit margin at 57.2% (+8.4 points YoY).

This is thanks to the accretive capex trends, as observed in the expanding ending total portfolio at 317 MW in FQ4'25 (+16.1% YoY) and the higher Monthly Recurring Revenue per Cabinet Equivalent Billed at $1,989 (+8.7% YoY).

With AMT already guiding data center revenue growth by +12.5% YoY in FY2026 against the +13.9% reported in FY2025 and the segment notably being a growth driver, it is unsurprising that they have allocated a higher capex at $695M in FY2026 (+4.4% YoY) with the new 55 MW under construction likely to be top/bottom-line accretive upon energization.

At the same time, there remains a great, multi-year growth runway, as observed in the 279 MW held for future development, allowing them to tap into the cloud super cycle through the expanded capacities/higher rates as similarly observed in the management's guidance of "CoreSite's achievement of mid-teens or higher stabilized yields on new data center deployments."

Profitable Growth Trends At Discounted Valuations

As a result of the robust FY2025 performance and the excellent adj FY2026 outlook, it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates remain promising, with AMT expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth prospect (FFO per share) at a CAGR of +3.6%/+7.9% through FY2028, respectively.

At the same time, the management has also hinted at their ability to drive further tower cash EBITDA margin expansion by up to 300 basis points through 2030, thanks to:

  • the planned land optimization/maintenance care of their international assets,
  • the implementation of a global, unified supply chain for an improved economy of scale, and
  • the streamlined technological platforms to "optimize customer service and accelerate automation,"

with it further lending credibility to their consistently rich adj EBITDA margins of 67% in FY2025 (in line YoY/+4 points from the 63% reported in FY2019).

Thanks to the recent correction, AMT looks even more compelling at FWD Price/FFO valuations of 18.05x and FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 16.22x, down from the 1Y mean of 19.66x/18.42x, the 5Y mean of 21.56x/21.48x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 21.50x/19.99x, respectively.

When comparing AMT's discounted FWD Price/FFO valuations of 18.05x and the consensus FFO per share growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +7.9% to its direct peers, including:

  • pure-play telecom REIT - CCI at 20.99x/+4.8%,
  • pure-play Data Center REIT - Digital Realty Trust (DLR) at 22.19x/+8.7%,
  • mixed Data Center/Storage REIT - Iron Mountain (IRM) at 25.77x/+11.4%,
  • pure-play Data Center REIT - Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) at 30.58x/+11.5%, respectively,

I am of the opinion that AMT's discounted valuations against the relatively robust FFO per share growth prospects have presented interested investors with a great dip buying opportunity here.

Readers must not ignore AMT's stable share count on a YoY basis and the relatively healthy net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 4.9x in FY2025 as well, compared to CCI at 8.34x, EQIX at 3.8x, IRM at 4.9x, and DLR at 4.9x, with it apparent that AMT continues to offer a compelling investment thesis.

These metrics position AMT well for their return to "accelerating, durable growth in 2027 and beyond," made sweeter by the recently raised dividends by +5.2%, the secure payouts at AFFO payout ratio of 65.62% (against the sector median of 74.07%), and the still rich yields at 4.05% compared to recent lows of 3.52% (against the sector median of 5.03%).

AMT Is An Even Better Buy Here

For now, the bulls have consistently defended AMT's 2024/2025/2026 trading floor of $170s, with it presenting great insights for those looking to add, especially since the pessimism embedded in its valuations is partly attributed to the DISH default.

Based on the FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 16.22x and the FY2025 AFFO per share performance at $10.76 (+8% YoY), it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value estimates of $174.50.

Based on the FWD Price/FFO valuations of 18.05x and the consensus FY2028 FFO per share estimates of $11.16, there remains a more than decent upside potential of +14.1% to my long-term, AMT price target of $201.40 as well.

This is not even taking into account the potential upgrading in its valuations nearer to the 5Y pre-pandemic Price/FFO valuation mean of 21.50x (still cheap compared to its peer group mean of 23.50x), potentially upon the recovery in market sentiments post DISH default, with it likely to trigger an expanded upside potential of +35.9% to my bull-case long-term price target of $239.90.

Combined with the still compelling/secure dividend investment thesis as discussed above, the management's confidence about delivering their prior organic growth rate at approximately +5% through 2027, and the great insights from the established trading floor at the $170s, I am reiterating my Buy rating for AMT stock here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Lionsgate: Will Continue To Roar
data.description:
In May 2024, the old Lions Gate Entertainment sold a ~12% stake in Lionsgate Studios (LION) to a SPAC for $10/share. In May 2025, Lions Gate distributed to shareholders one share of LION for every share of Lions Gate Entertainment they held. LION owns the movie and television production studios as well as the over 20,000 title film and television library, which includes the John Wick, Hunger Games, Twilight, Now You See Me, Knives Out, Mad Men, Ghosts etc.

Since the spin, LION has traded as low as $5.55 and as high as $11.02 recently, but generally below that $10 investment by the SPAC. Importantly, after the May 2025 spin, Liberty Strategic Capital (led by former Treasury Secretary Steve Mnuchin) increased its stake to 12.6% via open-market purchases. I believe these actions were one reason the company adopted a poison pill that expires in May of this year. Liberty bought more shares in Q3 to bring his stake to 12.94%, and in January, entered into a standstill arrangement whereby he would not buy more than 17.5% of the company for one year and Mnuchin was named to the Board of Directors. MHR, run by former Lions Gate Chairman Mark Rachevsky, owns 12.97%. Rachevsky is no longer on the board.

I first started looking at LION during the split from STRZ. (I wrote about STRZ for subscribers of my Investing Group service.) The Warner Brothers (WBD) saga refocused my attention on LION, specifically the value of its library. In my opinion, while Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount (PSKY) found WBD's production assets and the HBOmax streaming service valuable, a major draw was the purported 8,500 title movie and 50,000 title (over 5,000 separate series) television library. These include Warner Bros., HBO, Turner Entertainment (including pre-1986 MGM and other classics), New Line, Castle Rock, Hanna-Barbera and others. In an age where content is king, this library is a huge prize, one PSKY is willing to pay over 15x total EBITDA for.

My thesis was and continues to be that the WBD deal highlights the value embedded in LION. With WBD gone, LION becomes one of the largest if not the largest standalone library. Moreover, I believe it is highly likely that PSKY, the apparent winner of WBD, will not license any of the WBD library outside of the Paramount or HBOmax streaming services. That holdback should increase the value of any LION licenses to other services that need or want content.

Valuation:

WBD does not break out the revenue or profits from its library. LION does. In the most recent earnings release, the company announced that trailing twelve month library revenue was $1.05 billion, up 10% from the prior quarter's TTM results. The company has stated that library EBITDA margins are about 45%. Therefore, TTM library EBITDA was about $475 million. This EBITDA figure is considerably higher than the ~$250 million consensus EBITDA for the whole company for 2026.

I think it is silly to look at quarterly or even annual EBITDA of a movie/television studio's production department. First of all, production accounting is notoriously messy. Companies frontload production costs, which can make one quarter or year look terrible. Then, when the film or show, comes out, profits look artificially high. However, the beauty of this model is that once a movie or a show is a hit, it generates profits for years or decades. For example, it was reported that Netflix signed a five-year licensing deal north of $500 million for Seinfeld. Outside of residuals to writers, actors, etc, that $500 million goes straight to the pockets of the show's owners, Jerry Seinfeld and Larry David.

Therefore, I look at LION solely on the value of its library in an acquisition. I believe any acquirer would likely eliminate most of the corporate overhead and then milk the value of the library. I'm certain the production assets and teams have value, but this valuation method gives them to you for free.

Market Cap (@$9.65) $2.80 billion
Debt $1.922 billion
Minority Interest $144 million
Cash $182 million
Enterprise Value $4.685 billion
EV/TTM Library EBITDA ($475 million) 9.86x

As you can see above, the current value translates to a less than 10x multiple of TTM studio EBITDA. All other assets would be free. If you applied a 12x multiple to the studio EBITDA (a 20% discount to all of WBD), you would get another ~$3.50/share of value for LION (a little over $13/share).

Given the bidding war we just witnessed for WBD, if such a bidding war broke out for LION, 12x for the library sounds like a low multiple for me, particularly given the quality of the library and its recent production nature (85% produced since 2000). Every turn of that $474 million library EBITDA equals about $1.65 on the shares.

Odds of a Bidding War:

Now that the WBD saga has reach the first stage of its denouement with PSKY the apparent winner but not attained regulatory approval, I believe eyes can and might turn to LION. It is a large library of recently produced content that just grew 10% year over year. Unlike WBD, I believe it is also of a size that anti-trust would be a minor issue even for someone like Netflix. It is also small enough that a private equity-backed player like Legendary Films to explore buying the company.

Mnuchin's stake and knowledge of the company and industry makes him a contender to buy the whole business. I believe his presence and open market purchases are reasons why the company adopted the poison pill, which forces any acquirer to negotiate with the board. Should he not intend to purchase the company, Mnuchin's standstill agreement and seat on the board positions him for a say on who does purchase it and the price paid.

Risk:

The main trading risk here is that the WBD deal gets blocked somehow. If WBD falls, LION likely will as well even though the two don't have that much to do with one another. Another risk is operational. LION had some dud films last year and the year before, which hurt studio profits. Lastly, a pandemic or a writers/actors strike would hurt both production and movie attendance.

Conclusion:

I think LION is a unique asset that will become even more unique if WBD is purchased by PSKY. Even without that deal, however, I think the company can benefit from continued growth of its library and the cash that spits off as well and potential success of upcoming releases such as the upcoming Michael Jackson biopic. The shares can and likely will bounce around but I think there is more upside than down over time for the patient investor.
